enrichment.short_description: Tilda is a no-code website builder that uses pre-designed content blocks to let anyone create landing pages, online stores, blogs, and portfolios without writing code.
enrichment.seo_title: Tilda Review: Pricing, Features and Alternatives (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tilda is a block-based no-code website builder for landing pages, stores, and blogs. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tilda is a no-code website builder founded in 2014 by Nikita Obukhov in Moscow, Russia, and now operated by Tilda Publishing with a registered headquarters in London, United Kingdom. Rather than a blank canvas, Tilda is built around a library of more than 550 professionally designed content blocks that users assemble like a magazine layout, along with a freeform 'Zero Block' editor for fully custom designs. The product targets designers, marketers, small business owners, and agencies who want visually polished sites without hiring developers, and it supports landing pages, blogs, portfolios, online stores, and simple online courses. Tilda offers a genuinely usable free plan (one site, limited blocks), a Personal plan around $10 per month billed annually (single site, full block library, custom domain), and a Business plan around $20 per month billed annually (up to five sites plus HTML/CSS source code export). Monthly billing costs more than annual billing on both paid tiers. Key differentiators versus builders like Wix or Squarespace include Tilda's emphasis on editorial-style, magazine-quality layouts, built-in SEO controls (meta tags, structured data, redirects), fast static-style page delivery, and the ability to export static site code on the Business plan, which most closed-platform builders do not allow. It also includes native e-commerce tools (product catalogs, cart, and payment integrations) suitable for small stores, though it is less full-featured for commerce than dedicated platforms like Shopify. Tilda is popular in Europe and among design-conscious creators building marketing sites, one-page portfolios, and content-heavy landing pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tilda
paragraphs: Tilda is a no-code website builder that replaces the typical drag-and-drop canvas with a library of pre-designed content blocks. Instead of positioning individual elements freely, users stack and customize blocks for headers, image galleries, pricing tables, testimonials, and forms, which produces visually consistent, editorial-style pages faster than traditional builders. The company was founded in 2014 by designer and entrepreneur Nikita Obukhov in Moscow and has since grown into a globally used platform with a registered operating headquarters in London.

The platform is aimed at people who care about visual design but do not want to code: marketers, small business owners, freelance designers, agencies, and content creators. Tilda is used to build landing pages, corporate websites, blogs, online stores, digital magazines, and simple online courses. Its most distinctive feature is 'Zero Block,' a pixel-level freeform editor layered on top of the block system that lets more advanced users break out of the grid for fully custom designs while still benefiting from Tilda's hosting, SEO tools, and responsive engine.

Because pages are built from vetted design blocks rather than a blank canvas, Tilda tends to produce more visually cohesive results for non-designers than fully open canvas tools, at the cost of some layout flexibility compared to code-based development or highly flexible builders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tilda ships with over 550 ready-made content blocks covering headers, galleries, feature grids, pricing tables, forms, maps, and footers, all of which are responsive by default across desktop, tablet, and mobile. The Zero Block editor adds pixel-precise, freeform design control for users who want to move beyond the standard block grid without writing HTML or CSS.

Built-in SEO tools let users edit meta titles, descriptions, Open Graph tags, and URL structures per page, and the platform supports custom domains, SSL, and basic analytics integrations (Google Analytics, Facebook Pixel, and similar tracking tools). Tilda also includes native e-commerce functionality: product catalogs, a shopping cart, and integrations with payment providers, which makes it viable for small online stores without a separate platform.

On the Business plan, Tilda allows exporting a site's HTML/CSS source code, a feature most closed website builders (like Wix or Squarespace) do not offer, giving teams an exit path if they later want to host the static code elsewhere or hand it to developers for further customization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tilda's Free plan allows building one website with access to a limited set of basic blocks, useful for testing the platform or building a very simple page. The Personal plan costs $10 per month when billed annually (or $15 per month billed monthly) and unlocks the full block library, a custom domain, and all core platform features for a single site. The Business plan costs $20 per month billed annually (or $25 per month billed monthly) and supports up to five websites plus source code export.

All paid plans support Tilda's online store and course features, and annual subscribers can remove the 'Made on Tilda' footer branding. There is no enterprise or usage-based tier; pricing scales primarily by number of sites rather than by traffic or team size, which makes Tilda comparatively predictable for small teams and freelancers managing several client sites.

features.title: 550+ design blocks
features.description: A large library of professionally designed, responsive content blocks for headers, galleries, pricing, and more, assembled like a page layout tool.

features.title: Zero Block freeform editor
features.description: A pixel-level design canvas for building fully custom sections beyond the standard block grid, without writing code.

features.title: Built-in SEO controls
features.description: Per-page meta titles, descriptions, Open Graph tags, redirects, and clean URL structures for organic search visibility.

features.title: Native e-commerce
features.description: Product catalogs, shopping cart, and payment gateway integrations for running a small online store directly on Tilda.

features.title: Source code export
features.description: Business plan subscribers can export static HTML/CSS, giving an exit path other closed-platform builders typically lack.

features.title: Responsive design engine
features.description: Every block automatically adapts across desktop, tablet, and mobile breakpoints with per-device fine-tuning.

features.title: Custom domains and SSL
features.description: Connect a custom domain with free SSL on paid plans, plus support for DNS configuration and email forwarding.

features.title: Online course tools
features.description: Basic tools for structuring and selling simple digital courses or membership content directly from a Tilda site.

pros: Produces visually polished, magazine-quality pages quickly, even for non-designers
Genuinely usable free plan and low-cost entry-level paid tier compared to many competitors
Source code export on the Business plan is rare among no-code website builders
Strong built-in SEO controls for a no-code tool
Zero Block gives advanced users freeform design flexibility without leaving the platform
cons: Block-based approach can feel restrictive for users wanting a completely blank canvas
E-commerce features are basic compared to dedicated platforms like Shopify
Free and Personal plans limit users to a single website
Fewer native third-party app integrations than larger builders like Wix
Monthly (non-annual) billing carries a noticeable price premium

faqs.question: What is Tilda used for?
faqs.answer: Tilda is used to build landing pages, marketing and corporate websites, blogs, portfolios, small online stores, and simple online courses without writing code.

faqs.question: Is Tilda free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tilda has a free plan that allows building one website with a limited set of basic blocks. Full features require a paid Personal or Business plan.

faqs.question: How much does Tilda cost?
faqs.answer: The Personal plan starts at $10 per month billed annually (or $15 monthly), and the Business plan starts at $20 per month billed annually (or $25 monthly).

faqs.question: Who founded Tilda?
faqs.answer: Tilda was founded in 2014 by designer and entrepreneur Nikita Obukhov, originally in Moscow, Russia.

faqs.question: Can I export my Tilda website's code?
faqs.answer: Yes, on the Business plan users can export a site's HTML and CSS source code, which most closed no-code builders do not allow.

faqs.question: Does Tilda support e-commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tilda includes native product catalogs, a shopping cart, and payment integrations suitable for small online stores.

faqs.question: How is Tilda different from Wix or Squarespace?
faqs.answer: Tilda emphasizes editorial, block-based layouts and offers source code export on paid plans, while Wix and Squarespace lean more toward freeform canvases and closed hosting with no code export.

faqs.question: Is Tilda good for SEO?
faqs.answer: Tilda includes built-in controls for meta titles, descriptions, Open Graph tags, and clean URLs, making it a reasonably strong option for SEO-conscious site owners among no-code builders.

use_cases.title: Marketing landing pages
use_cases.description: Agencies and marketers use Tilda's block library to quickly launch campaign landing pages with polished, on-brand design.

use_cases.title: Portfolio and personal sites
use_cases.description: Designers, photographers, and freelancers use Zero Block and the block library to build visually distinctive personal or portfolio sites.

use_cases.title: Small online stores
use_cases.description: Small business owners use Tilda's built-in e-commerce blocks to sell a limited product catalog without adopting a separate storefront platform.

enrichment.short_description: Tiledesk is an open-source platform for building AI chatbots and combining them with live chat, letting support teams automate conversations across web, WhatsApp, and other channels.
enrichment.seo_title: Tiledesk Review: Open-Source AI Chatbot Platform Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tiledesk is an open-source AI chatbot and live chat platform. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and self-hosted vs cloud options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tiledesk is an open-source platform for building AI chatbots and blending them with human live chat agents, developed by Tiledesk S.r.l., an Italian company headquartered in Soleto, in the Puglia region of southern Italy. The company was formally founded in 2020 as a spin-off of the IT consultancy Frontiere 21, but its technical roots trace to 2017 with Chat21, an earlier open-source live messaging SDK the founding team built. Tiledesk positions itself as an 'AI Operating System' for customer conversations: teams design no-code chatbot flows and AI agents, deploy them on a website widget or channels like WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and Telegram, and hand conversations off to human agents when needed. A defining differentiator is that Tiledesk offers a genuinely open-source, self-hostable core (available on GitHub) alongside a hosted SaaS product, appealing to developers and privacy-conscious teams who want to run the platform on their own infrastructure rather than depend on a closed vendor. Cloud pricing starts with a free tier and paid plans beginning around EUR49 per month for a Starter tier (one user, roughly 800 conversations, a handful of AI agents and knowledge bases), scaling to a Pro tier around EUR149 per month with higher usage limits, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with unlimited users, agents, and SLA-backed support. Compared to closed live-chat and chatbot vendors like Intercom or Drift, Tiledesk competes primarily on openness, self-hosting flexibility, and lower entry cost, while lacking the brand recognition and marketing automation depth of larger incumbents.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tiledesk
paragraphs: Tiledesk is an open-source customer conversation platform that combines no-code AI chatbot building with live chat and multichannel messaging. It was built by Tiledesk S.r.l., an Italian software company headquartered in Soleto, Italy, formally founded in 2020 as a spin-off of the IT consultancy Frontiere 21, though the underlying technology dates back to 2017's Chat21 open-source messaging SDK.

The platform lets support and sales teams design chatbot conversation flows visually, connect them to large language model-powered AI agents and knowledge bases, and seamlessly escalate conversations to human agents when a bot cannot resolve a request. Conversations can be deployed through a website widget or connected to channels such as WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and Telegram from a single dashboard.

What sets Tiledesk apart from most commercial live-chat and chatbot vendors is that its core is genuinely open source and self-hostable, published on GitHub, so development teams can run the entire stack on their own servers instead of relying solely on Tiledesk's hosted cloud service. This makes it a common choice for companies with data residency requirements or developers who want to customize the platform's behavior directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tiledesk's chatbot builder is no-code, letting non-developers design conversation flows with conditional logic, and it integrates with AI agents and knowledge bases so bots can answer questions using a company's own documentation rather than generic responses. Live chat sits alongside the bot layer, with a shared inbox where human agents can take over any conversation a bot escalates.

The platform supports multichannel messaging, unifying conversations from a website widget, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and Telegram into one agent workspace. API-based integrations and webhooks allow teams to connect Tiledesk to CRMs, help desks, and internal tools, and the customizable chat widget can be styled to match a company's branding.

Because the core platform is open source, teams can self-host Tiledesk for full data control, or use the managed cloud version for a faster setup without infrastructure overhead. Both deployment paths share the same chatbot and live-chat feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tiledesk's hosted cloud service includes a free tier for testing, with paid plans starting at roughly EUR49 per month for the Starter tier, which includes one user, around 800 conversations per month, and a handful of AI agents and knowledge bases, backed by a 14-day free trial. The Pro tier costs roughly EUR149 per month and raises the limits to five users, dozens of AI agents, millions of AI tokens, and thousands of monthly conversations.

An Enterprise tier is available at custom pricing for organizations needing unlimited users, AI agents, conversations, and knowledge base items, along with SLA-backed support and a dedicated account manager. Because the platform is also open source, teams with development resources can avoid subscription costs entirely by self-hosting the core software, trading a monthly fee for their own infrastructure and maintenance effort.

features.title: No-code chatbot builder
features.description: Design AI-powered conversation flows visually without writing code, including conditional logic and escalation rules.

features.title: AI agents and knowledge bases
features.description: Connect chatbots to knowledge base content so AI agents can answer questions using a company's own documentation.

features.title: Live chat with bot handoff
features.description: Human agents can seamlessly take over any bot conversation from a shared team inbox.

features.title: Multichannel messaging
features.description: Unify conversations from a website widget, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and Telegram in one dashboard.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: Run the entire Tiledesk platform on your own infrastructure using the open-source codebase published on GitHub.

features.title: Customizable chat widget
features.description: Style the website chat widget to match brand colors, logo, and messaging tone.

features.title: API and webhook integrations
features.description: Connect Tiledesk to CRMs, help desks, and internal tools through its API and webhook support.

features.title: Conversation analytics
features.description: Track conversation volume, bot resolution rates, and agent performance to optimize support workflows.

pros: Genuine open-source, self-hostable core is uncommon among chatbot and live-chat vendors
Combines chatbot automation and live chat in a single, relatively affordable platform
Multichannel support for WhatsApp, Messenger, and Telegram alongside web chat
No-code flow builder lowers the barrier for non-technical teams
Free tier and lower entry pricing than many established live-chat competitors
cons: Smaller company and community than major players like Intercom or Zendesk
Self-hosting requires development and DevOps resources to maintain
Fewer native integrations and marketing automation features than larger incumbents
Conversation and AI usage limits can require upgrading plans quickly for active support teams
Documentation and support depth vary between the open-source and cloud versions

faqs.question: What is Tiledesk used for?
faqs.answer: Tiledesk is used to build AI chatbots and combine them with live chat and multichannel messaging for customer support and sales conversations.

faqs.question: Is Tiledesk open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tiledesk's core platform is open source and self-hostable, published on GitHub, alongside a managed cloud version.

faqs.question: How much does Tiledesk cost?
faqs.answer: Tiledesk offers a free tier, with paid cloud plans starting around EUR49 per month for the Starter tier and EUR149 per month for the Pro tier; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who founded Tiledesk?
faqs.answer: Tiledesk S.r.l. was founded in 2020 in Italy as a spin-off of the IT consultancy Frontiere 21, building on the earlier Chat21 open-source project started in 2017.

faqs.question: Can Tiledesk connect to WhatsApp?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tiledesk supports multichannel messaging including WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, and Telegram alongside its website chat widget.

faqs.question: Does Tiledesk support AI-powered chatbots?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tiledesk lets teams connect chatbots to AI agents and knowledge bases so bots can answer questions using company-specific content.

faqs.question: Where is Tiledesk headquartered?
faqs.answer: Tiledesk is headquartered in Soleto, in the Puglia region of southern Italy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between self-hosted and cloud Tiledesk?
faqs.answer: Self-hosted Tiledesk runs on a company's own infrastructure using the open-source code, while the cloud version is a managed SaaS product with subscription pricing and no infrastructure setup required.

use_cases.title: Automated customer support
use_cases.description: Support teams deploy AI chatbots to answer common questions instantly, escalating complex issues to live agents.

use_cases.title: Multichannel sales conversations
use_cases.description: Sales teams use Tiledesk to manage inbound leads across web chat, WhatsApp, and Messenger from a single inbox.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused self-hosted deployments
use_cases.description: Development teams self-host Tiledesk to keep customer conversation data on their own infrastructure for compliance reasons.

enrichment.short_description: TileServer GL is a free, open-source map tile server that hosts vector and raster maps with GL JSON styles, using server-side rendering by MapLibre GL Native.
enrichment.seo_title: TileServer GL: Free Open-Source Map Tile Server Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TileServer GL is an open-source vector and raster map tile server with GL JSON styles. Learn its features, setup, and self-hosting options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TileServer GL is a free, open-source map tile server for hosting vector and raster maps using GL JSON styles, with server-side raster rendering powered by MapLibre GL Native (a C++/OpenGL engine originally derived from Mapbox GL Native). It was first announced publicly at the FOSS4G 2016 conference as a project associated with Klokan Technologies, a Swiss company specializing in open-source geospatial software, and has since been maintained primarily under the MapTiler organization on GitHub (github.com/maptiler/tileserver-gl), with copyright credited across MapLibre contributors, MapTiler, Mapbox, and Klokan Technologies at various points in its history. It is licensed under the permissive BSD 2-Clause license. Unlike a commercial SaaS product, TileServer GL is not a company but a self-hosted software package: developers, GIS teams, and organizations run it on their own servers (or entirely offline) to serve map tiles for use in MapLibre GL JS, Mapbox GL JS, Leaflet, OpenLayers, and native Android/iOS mapping SDKs, as well as GIS clients via OGC WMTS. It typically consumes vector tiles produced by projects like OpenMapTiles and can rasterize them server-side into traditional PNG tiles, WMTS layers, or static map images as a fallback for clients that cannot render vector tiles directly. Because there is no vendor lock-in, licensing fee, or hosted account required, TileServer GL is popular for self-hosted mapping infrastructure, offline map deployments, and as an alternative to paid map-tile APIs from providers like Google Maps, Mapbox, or MapTiler Cloud. Node.js 20 or newer is required to run it, and it ships in both a full version (with native rendering dependencies) and a lightweight pure-JavaScript version that sacrifices server-side rasterization for easier portability.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TileServer GL
paragraphs: TileServer GL is an open-source map tile server built to host vector and raster maps styled with GL JSON, the JSON-based styling format originally defined by Mapbox and now used broadly across the open web mapping ecosystem. It was first announced at the FOSS4G 2016 conference and has been developed and maintained largely through the MapTiler organization on GitHub, with historical contributions credited to Klokan Technologies, Mapbox, and the MapLibre community. The project is licensed under the permissive BSD 2-Clause license and is free to download, modify, and self-host.

Unlike a hosted SaaS mapping API, TileServer GL is software that organizations run themselves, either on a cloud server, on-premises infrastructure, or entirely offline. It serves vector tiles (typically produced by projects like OpenMapTiles) directly to clients such as MapLibre GL JS, Mapbox GL JS, Leaflet, and OpenLayers for web use, and to native Android and iOS mapping SDKs for mobile apps, while also offering server-side rasterization for clients or GIS tools that require traditional raster tiles, WMTS layers, or static map images.

Server-side rendering is powered by MapLibre GL Native, a C++/OpenGL rendering engine accessed through Node.js bindings, which lets TileServer GL turn the same vector tile and style data into pixel-based images without requiring the client to do any GL rendering itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TileServer GL serves both vector tiles and server-rendered raster tiles from a single style definition, so the same map style can power interactive vector maps in modern JS libraries and static or legacy raster consumers (like older GIS clients) simultaneously. It supports OGC WMTS output for compatibility with traditional GIS software such as QGIS and ArcGIS.

The project ships in two forms: a full version with native rendering dependencies for server-side rasterization, and a lightweight pure-JavaScript version with no native dependencies that runs more portably (including on platforms where compiling native modules is difficult) but does not perform server-side rasterization. Both can be run via Docker or npm.

Because it is self-hosted and open source, TileServer GL gives organizations full control over map data, styling, and infrastructure costs, making it a common backend for offline mapping applications, custom basemaps, and self-hosted alternatives to commercial tile APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TileServer GL itself is free and open source under the BSD 2-Clause license; there is no subscription fee, tile-request pricing, or licensing cost from the project. Users only pay for the infrastructure they run it on (server or cloud compute costs) and, if desired, for source vector tile data such as OpenMapTiles extracts, which are separately licensed.

Organizations that prefer a managed alternative can use MapTiler's commercial hosted tile services or MapTiler Server product instead of self-hosting, but TileServer GL as a standalone open-source project remains free to run indefinitely with no vendor account required.

features.title: Vector and raster tile serving
features.description: Serves both vector tiles for modern GL-based clients and server-rendered raster tiles for legacy or GIS consumers from one style.

features.title: GL JSON styling
features.description: Uses the widely adopted GL JSON style format, compatible with Mapbox mobile SDKs, MapLibre, and ArcGIS Pro.

features.title: Server-side rasterization
features.description: Renders vector tiles into raster PNG tiles or static images on the server using the MapLibre GL Native engine.

features.title: OGC WMTS support
features.description: Exposes map layers via WMTS for compatibility with traditional GIS software like QGIS and ArcGIS.

features.title: Docker and npm deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker containers or npm packages, simplifying self-hosted deployment on any server.

features.title: Lightweight JS-only build
features.description: A pure-JavaScript version with no native dependencies for easier, more portable deployment without server-side rasterization.

features.title: Offline mapping support
features.description: Can serve fully offline map tiles, useful for embedded devices, field applications, or air-gapped environments.

features.title: Broad client compatibility
features.description: Works with MapLibre GL JS, Mapbox GL JS, Leaflet, OpenLayers, and native Android/iOS SDKs.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing fees
Full control over map data and infrastructure through self-hosting
Supports both modern vector-tile clients and legacy raster/GIS consumers
Works entirely offline, useful for embedded or restricted-network environments
Backed by an active open-source community and the MapTiler organization
cons: Requires self-hosting and server maintenance; there is no managed cloud offering built into the project itself
Server-side rasterization depends on native dependencies that can be harder to deploy on some platforms
No official commercial support without going through a third party like MapTiler
Requires separate acquisition of vector tile data (such as OpenMapTiles extracts) to have map content to serve
Setup and configuration require technical/DevOps familiarity compared to plug-and-play hosted map APIs

faqs.question: What is TileServer GL used for?
faqs.answer: TileServer GL is used to self-host vector and raster map tiles for web maps, mobile apps, and GIS software, serving as an alternative to commercial map tile APIs.

faqs.question: Is TileServer GL free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TileServer GL is free and open source under the BSD 2-Clause license, with no subscription or licensing fees from the project itself.

faqs.question: Who maintains TileServer GL?
faqs.answer: TileServer GL is maintained primarily through the MapTiler organization on GitHub, with historical contributions from Klokan Technologies and the broader MapLibre and Mapbox open-source communities.

faqs.question: What rendering engine does TileServer GL use?
faqs.answer: It uses MapLibre GL Native, a C++/OpenGL rendering engine, accessed via Node.js bindings, to rasterize vector tiles server-side.

faqs.question: Does TileServer GL work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it is self-hosted software, TileServer GL can run entirely offline or on isolated networks with locally stored tile data.

faqs.question: What map data does TileServer GL use?
faqs.answer: It typically serves vector tiles produced by projects like OpenMapTiles, though it can serve any compatible vector or raster tile source.

faqs.question: What are the system requirements for TileServer GL?
faqs.answer: Node.js version 20 or above is required, with Node 24 recommended; a lightweight pure-JavaScript version is also available with no native dependencies.

faqs.question: How is TileServer GL different from MapTiler's hosted services?
faqs.answer: TileServer GL is free, self-hosted software requiring your own infrastructure, while MapTiler's commercial cloud and MapTiler Server products are managed, paid alternatives for teams that don't want to self-host.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted web maps
use_cases.description: Development teams run TileServer GL to serve custom-styled maps for web applications without depending on a paid third-party tile API.

use_cases.title: Offline and embedded mapping
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy TileServer GL on local hardware to power maps in environments with no internet access, such as field devices or vehicles.

use_cases.title: GIS and enterprise mapping infrastructure
use_cases.description: Enterprises and government agencies use TileServer GL alongside OpenMapTiles data to build in-house mapping infrastructure with full data control.

features: No usage limits (subject to your own infrastructure)

enrichment.short_description: Time Doctor is a time tracking and employee monitoring platform for distributed and remote teams, offering activity tracking, screenshots, and productivity analytics.
enrichment.headquarters: Las Vegas, Nevada, United States (fully distributed team)
enrichment.seo_title: Time Doctor Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Time Doctor is a time tracking and employee monitoring tool for remote teams. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Time Doctor is a cloud-based time tracking and employee monitoring platform launched in 2012 by co-founders Rob Rawson and Liam Martin, built specifically to help manage and measure the productivity of distributed and remote teams. The company operates as a fully distributed organization with no central office, employing staff across more than 30 countries, and lists a nominal headquarters address in Las Vegas, Nevada. Time Doctor's core product tracks work time automatically, records periodic screenshots, monitors app and website usage, and generates productivity and attendance reports that managers use to evaluate remote employee output; it is frequently used by outsourcing agencies, BPOs, and remote-first companies to verify billable hours and monitor contractor activity. Pricing is per user per month: a Basic plan (around $7 to $8 per user monthly) covers core time tracking, projects, and screenshots; a Standard plan (around $10 to $14) adds attendance, time approvals, web/app usage reporting, and 60-plus integrations; a Premium plan (around $16.70 to $20) adds AI-driven benchmarking, video screen recording, SSO, and a dedicated success manager; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan adds private cloud deployment and guided implementation. Annual billing offers a meaningful discount over month-to-month pricing. Time Doctor differentiates itself from lighter time trackers like Toggl Track or Clockify through its heavier employee-monitoring feature set (screenshots, activity levels, mouse/keyboard tracking), positioning it closer to employee monitoring software like Hubstaff or ActivTrak than to simple freelancer time trackers, while competitors such as TimeCamp and Timely emphasize automatic or AI-based tracking with a lighter surveillance footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Time Doctor
paragraphs: Time Doctor is a time tracking and employee monitoring platform launched in 2012 by co-founders Rob Rawson and Liam Martin to help companies manage and measure the productivity of remote and distributed teams. The company itself operates as a fully distributed organization with employees spread across more than 30 countries and no central physical office, though it lists a corporate address in Las Vegas, Nevada.

The platform combines automatic time tracking with monitoring features such as periodic screenshots, activity level tracking, and website and application usage reports, giving managers visibility into how remote employees and contractors spend their working hours. It is widely used by outsourcing companies, BPOs, staffing agencies, and remote-first businesses that need to verify billable hours or oversee distributed contractor teams.

Time Doctor sits at the heavier-monitoring end of the time tracking market, alongside tools like Hubstaff, offering more surveillance-oriented features than lightweight time trackers such as Toggl Track, which appeals to companies prioritizing accountability and productivity oversight over a lighter-touch tracking experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Time Doctor's core feature set includes automatic and manual time tracking tied to projects and tasks, periodic desktop screenshots, and idle-time detection to flag inactive tracking sessions. Timeline reports break down a user's day by task and application, giving managers a granular view of how time was spent.

Higher-tier plans add attendance and schedule management, time-off tracking, payroll integration, and time approval workflows, along with web and app usage reports and productivity ratings that classify tools as productive or unproductive. The Premium plan introduces AI-driven features like Benchmarks AI (comparing performance across teams), an Unusual Activity report, mouse jiggler and clicker detection to catch tracking manipulation, and optional video screen recording.

Time Doctor integrates with more than 60 third-party tools including project management, payroll, and communication platforms, and offers a client login feature so agencies can give their own clients visibility into work performed on their behalf.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Time Doctor prices per user per month across three core self-serve plans: Basic at roughly $7 to $8 (monthly billing) or lower on annual billing, Standard at roughly $10 to $14, and Premium at roughly $16.70 to $20, with annual billing discounted compared to month-to-month. A custom-priced Enterprise plan adds private cloud deployment, customizable BI dashboards, and guided implementation for larger organizations.

All plans include a 14-day free trial with full access to Premium-tier features, and ten optional add-ons (such as Benchmarks AI, video screen recording, and SSO) let Basic and Standard customers selectively add higher-tier capabilities without upgrading their whole plan.

features.title: Automatic time tracking
features.description: Tracks work time automatically by project and task, with manual time entry also supported.

features.title: Screenshots and activity monitoring
features.description: Captures periodic desktop screenshots and tracks activity levels to verify active work during tracked time.

features.title: Web and app usage reports
features.description: Logs which websites and applications employees use during work hours and classifies them as productive or unproductive.

features.title: Attendance and scheduling
features.description: Tracks employee schedules, attendance, breaks, and leave requests alongside time data.

features.title: Payroll integration

faqs.question: What is Time Doctor used for?
faqs.answer: Time Doctor is used for time tracking and employee monitoring, primarily by remote teams, outsourcing agencies, and businesses that need to verify billable hours and productivity of distributed workers.

faqs.question: How much does Time Doctor cost?
faqs.answer: Time Doctor's Basic plan starts around $7 to $8 per user per month, Standard around $10 to $14, and Premium around $16.70 to $20, with lower rates on annual billing; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who founded Time Doctor?
faqs.answer: Time Doctor was founded in 2012 by Rob Rawson and Liam Martin.

faqs.question: Does Time Doctor take screenshots?
faqs.answer: Yes, Time Doctor captures periodic screenshots of employee screens as part of its activity monitoring, with settings that can be adjusted by administrators.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Time Doctor?
faqs.answer: Yes, Time Doctor offers a 14-day free trial that includes full access to Premium plan features.

faqs.question: How is Time Doctor different from Toggl Track?
faqs.answer: Time Doctor focuses heavily on employee monitoring with screenshots and activity tracking, while Toggl Track is a lighter-weight time tracker without surveillance-style monitoring features.

faqs.question: Does Time Doctor integrate with payroll systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Time Doctor integrates with payroll systems and generates payroll-ready reports based on tracked hours.

faqs.question: Is Time Doctor good for outsourcing and BPO companies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Time Doctor is widely used by outsourcing agencies and BPOs to verify contractor billable hours and monitor remote team productivity.

enrichment.short_description: TimeCamp is a time tracking and productivity platform offering automatic time tracking, invoicing, attendance, and budgeting for teams of any size, with a fully free plan for unlimited users.
enrichment.seo_title: TimeCamp Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TimeCamp is a time tracking tool with a free plan for unlimited users. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TimeCamp is a time tracking and productivity software platform founded in 2009 in Wroclaw, Poland by Kamil Rudnicki, and it remains best known in the market for offering a genuinely free plan with unlimited users, a rarity among competitors. The company, operating as Time Solutions / TimeCamp Inc., serves an international customer base (reportedly over 30,000 organizations) and maintains offices spanning Europe, North America, and Asia, with reported revenue around $3.8 million and roughly 4,000 paying customers as of 2024. TimeCamp automatically tracks time spent in applications and websites, attributes it to projects and tasks, and layers on attendance tracking, invoicing, budgeting, and billable-time reporting. Pricing starts free for unlimited users with core timesheet functionality, then scales through a Starter tier (around $2.99 to $3.99 per user monthly, annual billing) adding attendance and invoicing, a Premium tier (around $4.99 to $6.99) adding resource planning and budgets, and an Ultimate tier (around $7.99 to $9.99) adding SSO, custom roles, and unlimited integrations, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier offering self-hosted or private cloud deployment. TimeCamp differentiates itself from rivals like Toggl Track or Time Doctor primarily on price, aggressively undercutting them at the paid tiers while still including automatic tracking and screenshot-based monitoring on higher plans, making it a common choice for cost-conscious agencies, freelancers, and mid-size teams that want both time tracking and lightweight project budgeting in one tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TimeCamp
paragraphs: TimeCamp is a time tracking and productivity platform founded in 2009 in Wroclaw, Poland by Kamil Rudnicki. The company built its reputation around offering a fully free plan that supports unlimited users, a feature most competitors in the time tracking space do not match, which has made TimeCamp popular among small teams, freelancers, and budget-conscious agencies.

The platform automatically tracks time spent across desktop and web applications, attributing it to specific projects and tasks without requiring employees to manually start and stop timers for every activity. On top of time tracking, TimeCamp layers attendance management, invoicing, project budgeting, and billable-hours reporting, positioning it as a broader operations tool rather than a pure timer app.

TimeCamp serves an international base reportedly exceeding 30,000 organizations, with the company maintaining a presence across Europe, North America, and Asia. It competes directly with tools like Toggl Track, Clockify, and Time Doctor, generally differentiating on aggressive pricing at paid tiers while still delivering automatic tracking, screenshots, and integrations comparable to pricier competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TimeCamp's automatic time tracker runs in the background on desktop and mobile, detecting which applications and websites a user works in and mapping that activity to projects without manual timers. An AI Time Tracker feature is available even on the free plan, helping categorize tracked time automatically.

Paid tiers add attendance tracking and time-off management, invoicing generated directly from billable hours, project budgets and estimates, and a resource planner for allocating team capacity across projects. Higher tiers introduce screenshot monitoring, custom user roles, timesheet approval workflows, and unlimited third-party integrations.

TimeCamp integrates with a wide range of project management and accounting tools, supports data export for custom reporting, and, on its top Enterprise tier, offers self-hosted or private cloud deployment for organizations with strict data control requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TimeCamp's Free plan supports unlimited users and unlimited projects with core timesheets, desktop/mobile apps, and two-factor authentication, making it one of the most generous free tiers in the time tracking category. The Starter plan costs around $3.99 per user monthly (annual billing) or $5.49 monthly, adding attendance tracking, invoicing, and Excel report exports.

The Premium plan costs around $6.99 per user monthly (annual) or $9.99 monthly, adding resource planning, budgets and estimates, and app/website usage tracking. The Ultimate plan, TimeCamp's most popular tier, costs around $9.99 per user monthly (annual) or $13.99 monthly, adding screenshots, custom fields, SSO login, and unlimited integrations. A custom-priced Enterprise plan adds self-hosted deployment, audit logs, and advanced customization for large organizations.

Enterprise tier offers self-hosted deployment for data-sensitive organizations
cons: Interface and reporting can feel less polished than higher-priced competitors like Toggl Track

Integration depth on lower tiers is limited compared to Premium and Ultimate plans

Company size and brand recognition are smaller than category leaders

faqs.question: What is TimeCamp used for?
faqs.answer: TimeCamp is used for automatic time tracking, invoicing, attendance management, and project budgeting for freelancers, agencies, and teams.

faqs.question: Is TimeCamp free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TimeCamp offers a free plan that supports unlimited users and projects with core timesheet functionality; paid plans unlock invoicing, budgeting, and monitoring features.

faqs.question: How much does TimeCamp cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $3.99 per user per month (annual billing) for Starter, rising to $6.99 for Premium and $9.99 for Ultimate; Enterprise is custom-priced.

faqs.question: Who founded TimeCamp?
faqs.answer: TimeCamp was founded in 2009 in Wroclaw, Poland by Kamil Rudnicki.

faqs.question: Does TimeCamp track time automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, TimeCamp's desktop app can automatically detect application and website usage and attribute it to projects without manual timers.

faqs.question: Does TimeCamp support invoicing?
faqs.answer: Yes, TimeCamp can generate client invoices directly from billable tracked hours starting on the Starter plan.

faqs.question: How does TimeCamp compare to Toggl Track?
faqs.answer: TimeCamp is generally cheaper at comparable feature tiers and bundles invoicing and budgeting, while Toggl Track is known for a more polished interface and lighter feature focus on tracking alone.

faqs.answer: Yes, TimeCamp's Enterprise plan offers self-hosted or private cloud deployment for organizations with strict data control needs.

enrichment.short_description: Timely is an automatic, AI-powered time tracking app that uses its Memory Tracker to passively record work activity across apps, so teams do not need to manually start and stop timers.
enrichment.seo_title: Timely Review 2026: AI Time Tracking Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Timely is an AI-powered automatic time tracker built around its Memory feature. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Timely is an automatic, AI-powered time tracking application developed by Memory AS (also known as Memory.ai), a company founded in 2013 in Oslo, Norway by Mathias Mikkelsen. Its defining feature is Memory, an AI-driven background tracker that passively records every app, document, and website a user interacts with throughout the day, then reconstructs a private, editable timeline the user can turn into logged time entries without manually starting or stopping timers. This distinguishes Timely from timer-based competitors like Toggl Track or Harvest, and from heavier employee-monitoring tools like Time Doctor or Hubstaff, since Timely's tracking data stays private to the individual by default rather than being visible to managers for surveillance purposes. The company raised a $14 million funding round in 2021 to expand its AI-based productivity tooling and operates as a distributed team of roughly 40 people across around 20 countries, serving more than 5,000 businesses in over 160 countries, primarily consultancies, agencies, and professional services firms that bill clients by the hour. Pricing is per user per month: a Starter plan around $11 (capped at 5 users) covers automatic tracking, AI timesheets, and up to 20 projects; a Premium plan around $20 (up to 50 users) adds planned time tracking, team management, and budgeting; and an Unlimited plan around $28 covers unlimited users, capacity management, and multi-currency support, with an Unlimited+ tier for larger teams adding dedicated onboarding. Annual billing offers up to a 22% discount versus monthly billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Timely
paragraphs: Timely is an automatic time tracking application built by Memory AS, a Norwegian company founded in 2013 in Oslo by Mathias Mikkelsen. Rather than requiring users to start and stop timers for each task, Timely's core Memory feature runs quietly in the background, capturing every application, document, and website a person interacts with throughout the day and reconstructing a private, visual timeline of their work.

Users review that timeline and convert blocks of activity into logged time entries with a click, rather than remembering to track time in real time. Because the raw Memory data is private to each individual by default and only summarized time gets shared with managers or clients, Timely markets itself as a privacy-respecting alternative to heavier employee-monitoring software that captures screenshots or keystrokes.

The company raised a $14 million funding round in 2021 to expand its AI-driven productivity tooling and operates as a distributed team of roughly 40 people across about 20 countries, serving more than 5,000 businesses across 160-plus countries, with consultancies, agencies, and other client-billing professional services firms as its primary audience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Timely's Memory Tracker automatically records app usage, documents, meetings, and browser activity on Windows and macOS, using AI to group related activity into suggested time entries that users can approve, edit, or discard, eliminating the need for manual timers. AI Timesheets extend this by auto-generating draft timesheets from the recorded activity for faster weekly time approval.

A project dashboard gives visibility into billable hours, budgets, and team capacity in real time, while planned time tracking lets managers schedule and forecast work against project budgets ahead of time. Team management tools support capacity planning, overtime and undertime tracking, and multi-currency billing for agencies working with international clients.

Because raw activity data stays private to the individual, Timely emphasizes employee trust alongside accuracy, positioning Memory as a way to improve time-tracking completeness without turning tracking into surveillance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Timely's Starter plan costs around $11 per user per month (annual billing), capped at 5 users, and includes automatic time tracking, AI timesheets, a project dashboard, billable hours tracking, and support for up to 20 projects. The Premium plan costs around $20 per user per month, supports up to 50 users, and adds planned time tracking, team management, and budgeting and cost management tools.

The Unlimited plan costs around $28 per user per month and removes the user cap while adding capacity management, overtime/undertime tracking, and support for 50-plus currencies. Larger teams of 30 or more users can move to an Unlimited+ tier that adds dedicated onboarding and regular check-ins with a customer success team. Annual billing offers up to a 22% discount compared to monthly billing, and a 14-day free trial is available on all plans.

faqs.answer: Timely is used for automatic time tracking, primarily by consultancies, agencies, and professional services firms that bill clients by the hour.

faqs.question: How does Timely's Memory feature work?
faqs.answer: Memory runs in the background on macOS and Windows, using AI to record app, document, and browser activity and reconstruct a private, editable daily timeline that users convert into time entries.

faqs.question: How much does Timely cost?
faqs.answer: Timely's Starter plan costs around $11 per user per month, Premium around $20, and Unlimited around $28, all billed annually with a monthly option available at a higher rate.

faqs.question: Who founded Timely?
faqs.answer: Timely is developed by Memory AS, a Norwegian company founded in 2013 in Oslo by Mathias Mikkelsen.

faqs.question: Is Timely's tracking data private?
faqs.answer: Yes, raw Memory activity data is private to the individual user by default; only summarized time entries are visible to managers or shared with clients.

faqs.question: Does Timely have a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Timely offers a 14-day free trial across its plans.

faqs.question: How is Timely different from Time Doctor?
faqs.answer: Timely uses private, AI-driven automatic tracking without screenshots, while Time Doctor is a heavier employee-monitoring tool that captures screenshots and detailed activity data visible to managers.

faqs.answer: Yes, the Unlimited plan includes capacity management along with overtime and undertime tracking for team workload planning.

enrichment.short_description: Timescale is the company behind TimescaleDB, an open-source time-series database built as a PostgreSQL extension, plus a managed cloud database service now marketed under the Tiger Data brand.
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; usage-based plans from ~$30/month compute
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Timescale is the company behind TimescaleDB, an open-source Postgres time-series database. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Timescale is the company behind TimescaleDB, an open-source time-series database built as an extension to PostgreSQL rather than a separate database engine, letting developers get time-series and analytics performance while keeping standard SQL and the Postgres ecosystem. The company was founded in 2015 by CEO Ajay Kulkarni and CTO Michael Freedman, who met as MIT freshmen, and is headquartered in New York City with roughly 125 employees globally. Timescale has raised approximately $180 million in total funding, including a $110 million Series C led by Tiger Global alongside Benchmark, New Enterprise Associates, Redpoint Ventures, Icon Ventures, and Two Sigma Ventures, and was valued at over $1 billion following that round; the company reports roughly 2,000 customers and mid-eight-figure annual recurring revenue. In June 2025 the company rebranded from Timescale to Tiger Data, repositioning its cloud platform as 'the modern PostgreSQL for the analytical and agentic era' while keeping TimescaleDB as the name of its open-source Postgres extension. The core TimescaleDB extension remains free and open source, giving it a large developer user base, while the company generates revenue through its managed Tiger Cloud service, enterprise support, and premium features like automatic data compression, continuous aggregates, and tiered storage. Tiger Cloud pricing is consumption-based: compute is billed hourly starting around $30/month and storage is billed per GB-month (with unlimited tiered storage on higher plans), alongside a free tier for evaluation and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with 24/7 SLAs, HIPAA compliance, and SAML SSO. TimescaleDB competes with other time-series and analytical databases like InfluxDB, ClickHouse, and QuestDB, differentiating itself by staying fully SQL-compatible and embedded within the widely adopted PostgreSQL ecosystem rather than requiring a new query language or database engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Timescale
paragraphs: Timescale is the company behind TimescaleDB, an open-source time-series database built as a native extension to PostgreSQL rather than a standalone database engine. This design lets developers store and query time-series, events, and analytical data using standard SQL and the broader Postgres tooling ecosystem, while gaining performance optimizations purpose-built for time-ordered data such as automatic partitioning, compression, and continuous aggregation.

The company was founded in 2015 by CEO Ajay Kulkarni and CTO Michael Freedman, who met as MIT freshmen, and is headquartered in New York City with roughly 125 employees. Timescale has raised approximately $180 million in venture funding, including a $110 million Series C led by Tiger Global with participation from Benchmark, New Enterprise Associates, Redpoint Ventures, Icon Ventures, and Two Sigma Ventures, reaching a valuation over $1 billion.

In June 2025, the company rebranded its overall identity from Timescale to Tiger Data, repositioning its managed cloud platform as general-purpose modern PostgreSQL infrastructure for analytical and AI/agentic workloads, while retaining the TimescaleDB name for its widely used open-source Postgres extension.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TimescaleDB automatically partitions data into 'hypertables,' which behave like normal Postgres tables to applications but are internally chunked by time for fast ingestion and query performance at scale. Continuous aggregates precompute rollups (like hourly or daily averages) so dashboards and analytics queries stay fast even as raw data volume grows into the billions of rows.

Native compression can reduce storage footprint significantly for time-series data, and tiered storage automatically moves older, less-queried data to cheaper storage layers to control costs. Because it is a Postgres extension, TimescaleDB supports full SQL, existing Postgres extensions, and standard database tooling and drivers, avoiding the need to learn a new query language.

The managed Tiger Cloud service adds automated backups, high availability, read replicas, and enterprise security features like SAML SSO and HIPAA compliance, layered on top of the same open-source TimescaleDB engine used in self-hosted deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core TimescaleDB extension is free and open source and can be self-hosted on any PostgreSQL-compatible infrastructure at no licensing cost. The managed Tiger Cloud service uses consumption-based pricing: compute starts around $30/month (Performance/Developer tier) or $36/month (Scale tier), billed hourly based on usage, with storage billed per GB-month based on compressed data volume.

The Scale tier adds unlimited tiered storage at a lower per-GB rate, multi-node high availability, unlimited VPCs, and SOC 2 compliance reporting. A custom-priced Enterprise tier adds 24/7 production SLAs, HIPAA compliance, SAML SSO, cross-region backups, and larger instance sizes up to 64 CPU cores and 256 GB of memory per service. A free tier and 30-day free trial are available for evaluation before committing to paid usage.

features.title: Hypertables
features.description: Automatically partitions time-series data into time-based chunks while presenting a standard PostgreSQL table interface to applications.

features.title: Continuous aggregates
features.description: Precomputes and incrementally updates rollup queries so analytics dashboards stay fast at large data scale.

features.title: Native compression
features.description: Compresses time-series data to significantly reduce storage costs without sacrificing query performance.

features.title: Tiered storage
features.description: Automatically moves older, less-queried data to lower-cost storage tiers to control long-term storage spend.

features.title: Full SQL and Postgres compatibility
features.description: Runs as a native PostgreSQL extension, supporting standard SQL, drivers, and Postgres tooling without a new query language.

features.title: High availability and read replicas
features.description: Managed Tiger Cloud service includes automated backups, HA failover, and read replicas for production workloads.

features.title: Enterprise security
features.description: SAML SSO, HIPAA compliance, and SOC 2 reporting available on higher-tier managed plans.

features.title: Usage-based cloud pricing
features.description: Compute and storage are billed based on actual consumption rather than fixed instance sizes.

pros: Fully SQL-compatible, letting teams use existing PostgreSQL skills and tooling
Open-source core is free to self-host with no licensing cost
Strong compression and continuous aggregates for cost-efficient time-series analytics at scale
Backed by significant venture funding and a large, active open-source community
Managed Tiger Cloud service removes operational overhead for teams that don't want to self-host
cons: Consumption-based cloud pricing can be harder to predict than fixed-price database plans
Recent Timescale-to-Tiger Data rebrand may cause confusion during the transition period
Self-hosting still requires PostgreSQL operational expertise to manage effectively
Enterprise-grade compliance features (HIPAA, SAML SSO) are gated behind the custom-priced Enterprise tier
Competes in a crowded time-series database market against InfluxDB, ClickHouse, and QuestDB

faqs.question: What is Timescale used for?
faqs.answer: Timescale is used to store and query time-series, event, and analytical data at scale using PostgreSQL, commonly for IoT, monitoring, financial, and application analytics workloads.

faqs.question: Is TimescaleDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core TimescaleDB extension is open source and free to self-host; the managed Tiger Cloud service uses consumption-based pricing with a free tier available.

faqs.question: Who founded Timescale?
faqs.answer: Timescale was founded in 2015 by CEO Ajay Kulkarni and CTO Michael Freedman.

faqs.question: Did Timescale change its name?
faqs.answer: Yes, in June 2025 the company rebranded from Timescale to Tiger Data, while keeping TimescaleDB as the name of its open-source PostgreSQL extension.

faqs.question: How much does Timescale's cloud service cost?
faqs.answer: Tiger Cloud pricing is usage-based, with compute starting around $30 to $36 per month and storage billed per GB-month; a custom-priced Enterprise tier is also available.

faqs.question: Is TimescaleDB a separate database or a PostgreSQL extension?
faqs.answer: TimescaleDB is a native extension to PostgreSQL, not a separate database engine, so it supports standard SQL and Postgres tooling.

faqs.question: How much funding has Timescale raised?
faqs.answer: Timescale has raised approximately $180 million in total funding, including a $110 million Series C led by Tiger Global that valued the company at over $1 billion.

faqs.question: How does TimescaleDB compare to InfluxDB?
faqs.answer: TimescaleDB is built on PostgreSQL and supports full standard SQL, while InfluxDB uses its own query languages and storage engine; TimescaleDB is often preferred by teams wanting to stay within the Postgres ecosystem.

use_cases.title: IoT and sensor data analytics
use_cases.description: Companies use TimescaleDB to ingest and analyze high-volume sensor and IoT time-series data using familiar SQL.

use_cases.title: Application and infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: Engineering teams store metrics and observability data in TimescaleDB for fast dashboarding and alerting.

use_cases.title: Financial and analytical time-series data
use_cases.description: Financial and analytics teams use TimescaleDB's compression and continuous aggregates to query large historical datasets efficiently.

features: Limited compute and storage for evaluation
Up to 64 CPU / 256 GB per service

enrichment.short_description: TimeSolv is cloud-based time and billing software for law firms and professional services businesses, covering time tracking, invoicing, trust accounting, and payment collection.
enrichment.headquarters: West Palm Beach, Florida, United States
enrichment.seo_title: TimeSolv Review 2026: Legal Billing Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TimeSolv is legal time and billing software for law firms. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TimeSolv is cloud-based time and billing software built specifically for law firms and other professional services businesses that bill clients by the hour, providing time tracking, automated invoicing, trust accounting, and integrated electronic payment collection. The product dates back to 1999, originally developed under Thomson Reuters Elite Online Solutions before becoming privately owned in 2006, and has operated continuously since as one of the longer-standing dedicated legal billing platforms in the market. In 2020, TimeSolv was acquired by ProfitSolv, a portfolio holding company (backed by Lightyear Capital) that also owns other legal and professional-services software brands including Rocket Matter; TimeSolv's current headquarters and contact address is listed in West Palm Beach, Florida, alongside ProfitSolv's other operations. TimeSolv markets itself as the number one time and billing software for law firms, emphasizing that it helps firms track billable hours, automate invoice generation, and collect payments faster than manual processes. Pricing is per active user with no setup fees or long-term contract requirements: the TimeSolv Pro plan starts around $40 to $50 per user per month for firms up to about 9 users, and the TimeSolv Legal plan (with more law-firm-specific features such as trust accounting) costs more per user, with volume discounts available for larger firms and a standard 10-day free trial requiring no credit card. Add-on modules, including a client engagement/CRM tool and a website builder for law firms, are priced and sold separately from the core time and billing subscription. TimeSolv competes with other legal practice management and billing tools like Clio and Rocket Matter, generally differentiating on its long operating history and deep focus on time-and-billing rather than full practice management.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TimeSolv
paragraphs: TimeSolv is cloud-based time and billing software built specifically for law firms and other professional services businesses that bill clients by the hour. The product traces back to 1999, originally developed under Thomson Reuters Elite Online Solutions before becoming a privately owned company in 2006, making it one of the longer-running dedicated legal billing platforms still active in the market.

In 2020, TimeSolv was acquired by ProfitSolv, a portfolio company backed by Lightyear Capital that owns several legal and professional-services software brands, including practice management platform Rocket Matter. TimeSolv's current operations and contact address are based in West Palm Beach, Florida, alongside ProfitSolv's other brands.

The core product focuses on the financial operations of running a law firm: recording billable time, generating and sending client invoices, managing trust accounting for client retainers, and collecting payments electronically, rather than covering the full breadth of case or practice management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TimeSolv lets attorneys and staff track billable time against matters and clients from desktop, web, and mobile interfaces, then automates the invoice generation process based on recorded time, expenses, and firm-specific billing rules. It supports trust accounting for retainer and client fund management, a critical compliance requirement for law firms in many jurisdictions.

Integrated electronic payment processing lets firms collect client payments directly through TimeSolv invoices, and reporting tools give partners visibility into billable hours, realization rates, and outstanding accounts receivable across the firm. Optional add-on modules extend the platform, including a client engagement and CRM tool for intake and a website builder tailored to law firm marketing needs.

Because TimeSolv focuses specifically on time-and-billing rather than full case management, it is often used either as a standalone billing solution or alongside a separate practice/case management system for firms that need both.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TimeSolv prices per active user on a monthly or annual basis, with no setup fees and no long-term contract requirement beyond monthly billing. The TimeSolv Pro plan starts around $40 to $50 per user per month for firms of up to about 9 users, while the more feature-rich TimeSolv Legal plan, which includes law-firm-specific tools like trust accounting, is priced higher per user, with volume discounts available for firms with 5 or more users.

TimeSolv currently offers a promotion of 25% off the first year for annual plans (or 25% off the first four months for monthly plans), along with a 10-day free trial that requires no credit card. Add-on modules such as the client engagement/CRM tool (roughly $147 to $177 per month) and the website builder (roughly $149 to $159 per month) are billed separately from the core time and billing subscription.

features.title: Time and expense tracking
features.description: Records billable time and expenses against clients and matters from desktop, web, and mobile.

features.title: Automated invoicing
features.description: Generates client invoices automatically based on tracked time, expenses, and firm-specific billing rules.

features.title: Trust accounting
features.description: Manages client trust and retainer accounts to help firms meet legal and regulatory compliance requirements.

features.title: Electronic payment collection
features.description: Integrated payment processing lets clients pay invoices directly online, speeding up collections.

features.title: Billing and realization reporting
features.description: Reports on billable hours, realization rates, and accounts receivable to give firm leadership financial visibility.

features.title: Client engagement and CRM add-on
features.description: An optional module for managing client intake and engagement, sold separately from core billing.

features.title: Website builder add-on
features.description: An optional website builder tailored to law firm marketing needs, sold as a separate add-on.

features.title: Mobile time entry
features.description: Mobile apps let attorneys log billable time on the go rather than waiting until they're back at a desk.

pros: Long operating history (since 1999) with a platform purpose-built for legal billing
Trust accounting support addresses a key legal compliance requirement
Integrated payment processing speeds up client invoice collection
No setup fees or long-term contracts required, with a 10-day free trial
Backed by ProfitSolv's broader legal software portfolio for potential future integrations
cons: Focused specifically on time and billing rather than full case/practice management
Per-user pricing plus separately priced add-ons (CRM, website builder) can add up for growing firms
Pricing for core plans is not fully transparent without contacting sales for volume quotes
Smaller company scale than broader legal tech platforms like Clio
Firms needing full practice management may need to pair TimeSolv with a separate system

faqs.question: What is TimeSolv used for?
faqs.answer: TimeSolv is used by law firms and other professional services businesses for time tracking, automated invoicing, trust accounting, and collecting client payments.

faqs.question: How much does TimeSolv cost?
faqs.answer: The TimeSolv Pro plan starts around $40 to $50 per user per month for firms up to about 9 users, with the more feature-rich TimeSolv Legal plan priced higher and volume discounts for larger firms.

faqs.question: When was TimeSolv founded?
faqs.answer: TimeSolv's product dates back to 1999, originally developed under Thomson Reuters Elite Online Solutions before becoming a privately owned company in 2006.

faqs.question: Who owns TimeSolv?
faqs.answer: TimeSolv was acquired by ProfitSolv, a portfolio company backed by Lightyear Capital, in 2020; ProfitSolv also owns other legal software brands including Rocket Matter.

faqs.question: Does TimeSolv support trust accounting?
faqs.answer: Yes, TimeSolv includes trust accounting functionality to help law firms manage client retainer funds in compliance with legal requirements.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for TimeSolv?
faqs.answer: Yes, TimeSolv offers a 10-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: Does TimeSolv include full legal practice management?
faqs.answer: No, TimeSolv focuses specifically on time and billing rather than full case or practice management, though it offers optional add-ons like a client engagement/CRM tool.

faqs.question: Where is TimeSolv headquartered?
faqs.answer: TimeSolv's current headquarters and contact address is listed in West Palm Beach, Florida, alongside its parent company ProfitSolv.

use_cases.title: Law firm billable time and invoicing
use_cases.description: Law firms use TimeSolv to track attorney billable hours and automatically generate accurate client invoices.

use_cases.title: Trust account compliance
use_cases.description: Firms use TimeSolv's trust accounting tools to manage client retainer funds in line with legal compliance requirements.

use_cases.title: Professional services hourly billing
use_cases.description: Consulting and other professional services firms that bill hourly use TimeSolv for time tracking and invoicing outside the legal industry.
